title: Merchandise exports by continent of destination
subtitle: Figures correspond to the value of [merchandise exports](#dod:merchandise-exports) by continental destination as a share of [gross domestic product](#dod:gdp) (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.
caption:
Merchandise exports by continent of destination

Figures correspond to the value of merchandise exports by continental destination as a share of gross domestic product (GDP). All partner countries are classified into continent groupings according to OWID's classification.

The stacked area chart tracks the value of India's merchandise exports to seven continental regions as a share of gross domestic product from 1949 to 2014. The vertical axis measures the cumulative percentage of GDP, ranging from 0% to 18%, while the horizontal axis spans the 65-year period. The data series are stacked in the following order from bottom to top: Exports to Asia, Exports to Western Europe, Exports to North America, Exports to South America, Exports to Africa, Exports to Eastern Europe, and Exports to Oceania. The estimates derive from dyadic transactions data, where intra-regional trade, such as Western Europe to Western Europe, is calculated as the sum of exports between those countries divided by the region's GDP. Exports to Asia form the largest component throughout the timeline, starting near 2% in 1949, dipping below 1% in the late 1960s, and rising steadily to occupy the space between 0% and roughly 8% by 2014. Exports to Western Europe constitute the second-largest layer, visible as a maroon band that expands significantly after 1990. The green band representing North America and the orange band for Africa show moderate growth, particularly in the later decades. The upper layers for South America, Eastern Europe, and Oceania remain thin throughout the period. The total height of the stacked areas shows a general upward trajectory, beginning around 4% in 1949, experiencing volatility with a peak near 8% in the early 1950s, and reaching a maximum value exceeding 16% in the years leading up to 2014. The cumulative share of exports to all continents rises from the mid-single digits in the mid-20th century to over 16% of GDP by the end of the observed period.
